title.zh: 卷三十五 荊燕吳傳
title.en: Volume 35: Princes of Jing, Yan and Wu
title.raw: 卷三十五 荊燕吳傳 Volume 35: Princes of Jing, Yan and Wu

sentences.zh: 漢王敗成皋，北度河，得張耳、韓信軍，軍脩武，深溝高壘，使賈將二萬人，騎數百，擊楚，度白馬津入楚地，燒其積聚，以破其業，無以給項王軍食。

translations.literal: When the King of Han was defeated at Chenggao, he crossed the Yellow River to the north, took command of the armies of Zhang Er and Han Xin, and encamped at Xiuwu, digging deep moats and raising high ramparts. He sent Liu Jia with twenty thousand infantry and several hundred horsemen to strike Chu. Liu Jia crossed the Baima Ford into Chu territory and burned their accumulated stores, wrecking their supply lines so that nothing could be fed to King Xiang’s army.
translations.idiomatic: After his defeat at Chenggao, the King of Han withdrew north across the river and joined the forces of Zhang Er and Han Xin. They made camp at Xiuwu behind deep moats and high walls. He dispatched Liu Jia with twenty thousand foot soldiers and several hundred cavalry to hit Chu from the flank. Liu Jia crossed the Baima Ford into Chu lands and torched their depots, shattering their logistics so that King Xiang’s troops went hungry.

sentences.zh: 已而楚兵擊之，賈輒避不肯與戰，而與彭越相保。

translations.literal: Soon afterward Chu troops attacked him; Liu Jia always avoided battle and refused to engage, instead keeping his forces sheltered with Peng Yue.
translations.idiomatic: When Chu forces moved against him, Liu Jia consistently declined combat and kept his army under Peng Yue’s protection.

sentences.zh: 漢王追項籍至固陵，使賈南度淮圍壽春。

translations.literal: The King of Han pursued Xiang Ji to Guling and sent Liu Jia south across the Huai to besiege Shouchun.
translations.idiomatic: Pursuing Xiang Yu as far as Guling, the King of Han ordered Liu Jia south across the Huai to invest Shouchun.

sentences.zh: 還至，使人間招楚大司馬周殷。

translations.literal: On his return he sent men in secret to win over Zhou Yin, the Great Marshal of Chu.
translations.idiomatic: When he came back he dispatched agents to sound out Zhou Yin, Chu’s grand marshal, in private.

sentences.zh: 周殷反楚，佐賈舉九江，迎英布兵，皆會垓下，誅項籍。

translations.literal: Zhou Yin turned against Chu, aided Liu Jia in raising Jiujiang, welcomed Ying Bu’s troops, and they all converged at Gaixia, where Xiang Ji was executed.
translations.idiomatic: Zhou Yin rebelled against Chu, helped Liu Jia secure Jiujiang, joined with Ying Bu’s army, and the allied hosts met at Gaixia to destroy Xiang Yu.

sentences.zh: 漢王因使賈將九江兵，與太尉盧綰西南擊臨江王共尉，尉死，以臨江為南郡。

translations.literal: The King of Han therefore ordered Liu Jia to lead the Jiujiang troops. Together with Grand Commandant Lu Wan he attacked King Gong Wei of Linjiang from the southwest. Gong Wei died, and Linjiang was reorganized as Nan Commandery.
translations.idiomatic: The King of Han then put Liu Jia in command of the Jiujiang forces. With Grand Commandant Lu Wan he struck southwest at Gong Wei, king of Linjiang. Gong Wei fell, and his domain was converted into Nan Commandery.

sentences.zh: 賈既有功，而高祖子弱，昆弟少，又不賢，欲王同姓以填天下，乃下詔曰：「將軍劉賈有功，及擇子弟可以為王者。」

translations.literal: Liu Jia had already earned great merit, but the Gaozu’s sons were still young, his brothers were few, and none showed real talent, so he wished to invest princes of his own surname to secure the realm. He therefore issued an edict: “General Liu Jia has rendered outstanding service; now select from among the imperial nephews one fit to be king.”
translations.idiomatic: Liu Jia stood high in merit, yet the founder’s sons were weak, his brothers scarce, and none of them promising enough to hold the frontier. Gaozu meant to plant Liu kinsmen as kings to steady the empire, and proclaimed: “General Liu Jia has distinguished himself; choose from the younger generation of the house whom we may enfeoff as king.”

sentences.zh: 群臣皆曰：「立劉賈為荊王，王淮東。」

translations.literal: The assembled ministers all said, “Establish Liu Jia as King of Jing, ruling the territory east of the Huai.”
translations.idiomatic: The ministers replied in one voice: “Make Liu Jia king of Jing with his seat east of the Huai.”

sentences.zh: 立六年而淮南王黥布反，東擊荊。

translations.literal: Six years after his enthronement, Qing Bu, king of Huainan, rebelled and marched east against Jing.
translations.idiomatic: In the sixth year of his reign Qing Bu, the tattooed king of Huainan, rose in revolt and drove east into Jing.

sentences.zh: 賈與戰，弗勝，走富陵，為布軍所殺。

translations.literal: Liu Jia gave battle but was defeated, fled to Fuling, and was killed by Qing Bu’s army.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Jia engaged him but could not prevail. He retreated to Fuling, where Qing Bu’s troops cut him down.

sentences.zh: 燕王劉澤，高祖從祖昆弟也。

translations.literal: King Liu Ze of Yan was a second cousin of Gaozu on the male line (they shared the same great-grandfather).
translations.idiomatic: Liu Ze, king of Yan, was the founder’s patrilateral second cousin.

sentences.zh: 高祖三年，澤為郎中。

translations.literal: In Gaozu’s third year, Liu Ze served as gentleman of the palace.
translations.idiomatic: In the third year of Gaozu’s reign Liu Ze was appointed gentleman of the palace.

sentences.zh: 十一年，以將軍擊陳豨將王黃，封為營陵侯。

translations.literal: In the eleventh year, as a general he attacked Wang Huang, a commander under Chen Xi, and was enfeoffed marquis of Yingling.
translations.idiomatic: In the eleventh year he campaigned as a general against Wang Huang, one of Chen Xi’s officers, and received the marquisate of Yingling.

sentences.zh: 高后時，齊人田生游乏資，以畫奸澤。

translations.literal: During the reign of Empress Gao, a man of Qi named Tian Sheng traveled in reduced circumstances and, short of funds, importuned Liu Ze with a crafty scheme.
translations.idiomatic: Under Empress Dowager Lü, the Qi native Tian Sheng wandered the capital in want of money and approached Liu Ze with a subtle plan.

sentences.zh: 澤大說之，用金二百斤為田生壽。

translations.literal: Liu Ze was delighted and presented him with two hundred catties of gold as a birthday gift.
translations.idiomatic: Delighted, Liu Ze gave him two hundred catties of gold to toast his longevity.

sentences.zh: 田生已得金，即歸齊。

translations.literal: Tian Sheng took the gold and returned to Qi.
translations.idiomatic: Tian Sheng pocketed the gold and went home to Qi.

sentences.zh: 二歲，澤使人謂田生曰：「弗與矣。」

translations.literal: Two years later Liu Ze sent word to Tian Sheng: “There will be no more gifts.”
translations.idiomatic: After two years Liu Ze sent a message: “The stipends stop here.”

sentences.zh: 田生如長安，不見澤，而假大宅，令其子求事呂后所幸大謁者張卿。

translations.literal: Tian Sheng went to Chang’an but avoided Liu Ze. Instead he leased a grand mansion and had his son seek a post under Zhang Qing, the chief usher whom Empress Dowager Lü favored.
translations.idiomatic: Tian Sheng traveled to Chang’an, never called on Liu Ze, rented a large house, and arranged for his son to enter service under Zhang Qing, the great usher in the empress dowager’s intimate circle.

sentences.zh: 居數月，田生子請張卿臨，親脩具。

translations.literal: Several months later Tian Sheng’s son invited Zhang Qing to visit and personally prepared the feast.
translations.idiomatic: Months later the son invited Zhang Qing to dinner and oversaw every detail of the banquet himself.

sentences.zh: 張卿往，見田生帷帳具置如列侯。

translations.literal: Zhang Qing came and saw curtains and vessels arrayed as for a full marquis.
translations.idiomatic: When Zhang Qing arrived he found hangings and service fit for a marquis of the first rank.

sentences.zh: 張卿驚。

translations.literal: Zhang Qing was startled.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Qing was taken aback.

sentences.zh: 酒酣，乃屏人說張卿曰：「臣觀諸侯邸第百餘，皆高帝一切功臣。

translations.literal: When the wine had flowed freely, Tian Sheng dismissed the attendants and said to Zhang Qing, “I have inspected the mansions of more than a hundred nobles; all belong to Gaodi’s founding ministers enfeoffed in a single stroke at the empire’s beginning.”
translations.idiomatic: When they were deep in their cups, Tian Sheng sent the servants away and said, “I have walked the lanes of over a hundred noble houses. Every one belongs to the merit ministers whom Gaozu enfeoffed wholesale at the founding.”

sentences.zh: 今呂氏雅故本推轂高帝就天下，功至大，又有親戚太后之重。

translations.literal: “The Lü clan from the outset turned the wheels for Gaozu in winning the realm; their service is unmatched, and they also hold the empress dowager’s blood ties.”
translations.idiomatic: “The Lüs have from the first pushed the founder’s chariot to empire; their merit is supreme, and they are the empress dowager’s own kin besides.”

sentences.zh: 太后春秋長，諸呂弱，太后欲立呂產為呂王，王代。

translations.literal: “The empress dowager is advanced in years, the Lü men are still weak, and she wishes to make Lü Chan king of the Lü domain and invest him with Dai.”
translations.idiomatic: “The empress dowager is old, her Lü nephews still young in power, and she means to raise Lü Chan as king of the Lü line with the territory of Dai.”

sentences.zh: 呂后又重發之，恐大臣不聽。

translations.literal: “Yet she hesitates to bring the matter forward, fearing the high ministers will not consent.”
translations.idiomatic: “She dare not press it openly for fear the great ministers will balk.”

sentences.zh: 今卿最幸，大臣所敬，何不風大臣以聞太后，太后必喜。

translations.literal: “You stand highest in her favor and are respected at court. Why not prompt the ministers to memorialize her? She will be pleased.”
translations.idiomatic: “You enjoy her deepest trust and the ministers’ deference. Hint to the ministers that they should memorialize the throne—she will rejoice at it.”

sentences.zh: 諸呂以王，萬戶侯亦卿之有。

translations.literal: “Once the Lü are kings, you may expect to be raised to marquis over ten thousand households yourself.”
translations.idiomatic: “When the Lü princes are enthroned, a ten-thousand-household marquisate will be yours as well.”

sentences.zh: 太后心欲之，而卿為內臣，不急發，恐過及身矣。」

translations.literal: “Her heart is set on this; as her intimate courtier, if you do not move quickly, disaster may reach your own person.”
translations.idiomatic: “This is what she longs for, and you are her inside man. If you delay, misfortune may fall on you first.”

sentences.zh: 張卿大然之，乃風大臣語太后。

translations.literal: Zhang Qing agreed wholeheartedly and quietly urged the leading ministers to speak to the empress dowager.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Qing was convinced and privately coached the high ministers to approach the empress dowager.

sentences.zh: 太后朝，因問大臣。

translations.literal: At the next court audience she questioned the ministers on the point.
translations.idiomatic: When she held court she put the question to them directly.

sentences.zh: 大臣請立呂產為呂王。

translations.literal: The ministers requested that Lü Chan be made king of the Lü line.
translations.idiomatic: They asked that Lü Chan be established as king of the Lü domain.

sentences.zh: 太后賜張卿千金，張卿以其半進田生。

translations.literal: The empress dowager rewarded Zhang Qing with a thousand catties of gold, and he gave half of it to Tian Sheng.
translations.idiomatic: She bestowed a thousand catties of gold on Zhang Qing, who forwarded half to Tian Sheng.

sentences.zh: 田生弗受，因說之曰：「呂產王也，諸大臣未大服。

translations.literal: Tian Sheng refused the gift and offered further counsel: “Even after Lü Chan is made king, many ministers will still be only half persuaded.”
translations.idiomatic: Tian Sheng declined the gold and argued, “Lü Chan’s kingship will not yet win the full submission of the great ministers.”

sentences.zh: 今營陵侯澤，諸劉長，為大將軍，獨此尚觖望。

translations.literal: “Marquis Liu Ze of Yingling is the senior among the Liu princes and now serves as grand general; he alone nurses resentment at being passed over.”
translations.idiomatic: “Liu Ze of Yingling is the eldest of the Liu princes and holds the post of grand general; he is the one who still smarts at slight.”

sentences.zh: 今卿言太后，裂十餘縣王之，彼得王喜，於諸呂王益固矣。」

translations.literal: “If you tell the empress dowager to carve out more than ten counties for his kingdom, his satisfaction will bind the Liu faction more firmly behind the new Lü kings.”
translations.idiomatic: “Ask her to split off a dozen counties for his kingdom. Once he has a crown of his own, he will swallow his grievance and the Lü kingships will stand on firmer ground.”

sentences.zh: 張卿入言之。

translations.literal: Zhang Qing relayed this to the throne.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Qing carried the plea inward.

sentences.zh: 又太后女弟呂須女亦為營陵侯妻，故遂立營陵侯澤為琅邪王。

translations.literal: Moreover the empress dowager’s younger sister Lü Xu’s daughter was also wife to Marquis Liu Ze of Yingling; therefore Liu Ze of Yingling was at last established as king of Langye.
translations.idiomatic: The empress dowager’s younger sister Lü Xu had given her daughter in marriage to Marquis Liu Ze of Yingling, which is why Liu Ze was finally raised to king of Langye.

sentences.zh: 琅邪王與田生之國，急行毋留。

translations.literal: The new king of Langye and Tian Sheng set out for their domain at full speed and did not linger.
translations.idiomatic: The king of Langye and Tian Sheng rode post-haste to their fief and would not dawdle.

sentences.zh: 出關，太后果使人追之。

translations.literal: When they had left the pass, the empress dowager still sent men in pursuit.
translations.idiomatic: They cleared the barrier; she sent riders after them all the same.

sentences.zh: 已出，即還。

translations.literal: Once they were already out, the pursuers turned back.
translations.idiomatic: By the time the pursuit caught up they were gone, and the chase was abandoned.

sentences.zh: 吳王濞

translations.literal: Prince of Wu Bi.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Bi, prince of Wu.

sentences.zh: 吳王濞，高帝兄仲之子也。

translations.literal: Liu Bi, prince of Wu, was the son of Liu Zhong, elder brother to Gaozu.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Bi, king of Wu, was the son of Liu Zhong, the founder’s elder brother.

sentences.zh: 高帝立仲為代王。

translations.literal: Gaozu had first enfeoffed Liu Zhong as king of Dai.
translations.idiomatic: The founder first made Liu Zhong king of Dai.

sentences.zh: 匈奴攻代，仲不能堅守，棄國間行，走雒陽，自歸，天子不忍致法，廢為合陽侯。

translations.literal: When the Xiongnu attacked Dai, Liu Zhong could not hold his ground. He abandoned his kingdom, traveled in secret to Luoyang, and surrendered himself. The Son of Heaven could not bear to punish him harshly, so he was demoted to marquis of Heyang.
translations.idiomatic: The nomads struck Dai; Liu Zhong failed to defend it. He slipped away to Luoyang and gave himself up. The emperor spared him the full penalty and reduced him to marquis of Heyang.

sentences.zh: 子濞，封為沛侯。

translations.literal: His son Liu Bi was enfeoffed marquis of Pei.
translations.idiomatic: His son Liu Bi received the marquisate of Pei.

sentences.zh: 黥布反，高祖自將往誅之。

translations.literal: When Qing Bu rebelled, Gaozu took the field in person to destroy him.
translations.idiomatic: When Qing Bu rose, Gaozu marched in person to crush the revolt.

sentences.zh: 濞年二十，以騎將從破布軍。

translations.literal: Twenty-year-old Liu Bi served as a cavalry commander in the army that defeated Qing Bu.
translations.idiomatic: At twenty Liu Bi rode as a cavalry general in the host that broke Qing Bu.

sentences.zh: 荊王劉賈為布所殺，無後。

translations.literal: King Liu Jia of Jing had been killed by Qing Bu and left no heir.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Jia, king of Jing, had fallen to Qing Bu and had no successor.

sentences.zh: 上患吳會稽輕悍，無壯王填之，諸子少，乃立濞於沛，為吳王，王三郡五十三城。

translations.literal: The emperor worried that the people of Wu and Kuaiji were light and fierce and needed a strong prince to govern them; his own sons were still young, so he raised Liu Bi from Pei to be king of Wu over three commanderies and fifty-three cities.
translations.idiomatic: The throne feared the martial temper of Wu and Kuaiji and wanted a mature prince to hold them, yet the imperial sons were young. Liu Bi was therefore elevated from Pei to king of Wu with three commanderies and fifty-three towns.

sentences.zh: 已拜受印，高祖召濞相之，曰：「若狀有反相。」

translations.literal: After the investiture rites Gaozu examined Liu Bi’s face and said, “Your features carry the mark of one who would revolt.”
translations.idiomatic: When the seal had been delivered Gaozu studied his face and said, “You have the look of a rebel.”

sentences.zh: 獨悔，業已拜，因拊其背，曰：「漢後五十年東南有亂，豈若邪？

translations.literal: He regretted it at once, but the patent had already been granted, so he clapped Liu Bi on the back and said, “Fifty years hence there will be turmoil in the southeast of the Han realm—will that be your doing?
translations.idiomatic: He repented immediately, yet the appointment stood, so he patted Liu Bi’s back and said, “Within fifty years the southeast will rise in strife—will it be you?

sentences.zh: 然天下同姓一家，慎無反！」

translations.literal: Yet the empire is one house of the Liu name; see that you never think of rebellion!”
translations.idiomatic: Still, the realm is one Liu clan; do not dare revolt!”

sentences.zh: 濞頓首曰：「不敢。」

translations.literal: Liu Bi kowtowed and said, “I would not dare.”
translations.idiomatic: Liu Bi touched his forehead to the ground and swore he would not.

sentences.zh: 會孝惠、高后時天下初定，郡國諸侯各務自拊循其民。

translations.literal: During the reigns of Emperor Xiaohui and Empress Gao, when the land was newly pacified, every prince and marquis devoted himself to comforting his people.
translations.idiomatic: In the Xiaohui and Empress Gao years, with the realm just settled, each regional lord strove to win the goodwill of his subjects.

sentences.zh: 吳有豫章郡銅山，即招致天下亡命者盜鑄錢，東煮海水為鹽，以故無賦，國用饒足。

translations.literal: Wu controlled the copper mountains of Yuzhang and summoned fugitives from across the realm to mint coin in secret; eastward it boiled seawater for salt. It therefore levied no grain tax, and the royal treasury overflowed.
translations.idiomatic: Wu held the Yuzhang copper pits and drew runaways from every quarter to cast money illicitly; along the coast it boiled brine for salt. On that income it waived ordinary levies and the kingdom grew fabulously rich.

sentences.zh: 孝文時，吳太子入見，得侍皇太子飲博。

translations.literal: Under Emperor Xiaowen the Wu heir came to court and was admitted to drink and play pitch-pot with the heir apparent.
translations.idiomatic: In Wendi’s reign the crown prince of Wu attended court and was allowed to drink and gamble on pitch-pot with the imperial heir.

sentences.zh: 吳太子師傅皆楚人，輕悍，又素驕。

translations.literal: The Wu heir’s tutors were men of Chu, themselves rash and martial, and they had bred arrogance in their charge.
translations.idiomatic: His tutors were Chu men, hot-blooded by nature, and they had spoiled him into insolence.

sentences.zh: 博爭道，不恭，皇太子引博局提吳太子，殺之。

translations.literal: A dispute over a move in the game turned disrespectful; the imperial heir snatched up the gaming board and dashed it against the Wu prince, killing him.
translations.idiomatic: They quarreled over the rules; the Wu heir showed disrespect, and the crown prince seized the heavy board and struck him dead.

sentences.zh: 於是遣其喪歸葬吳。

translations.literal: The court therefore sent the corpse back to Wu for burial.
translations.idiomatic: The court had the body sent home to Wu for burial.

sentences.zh: 吳王慍曰：「天下一宗，死長安即葬長安，何必來葬！」

translations.literal: The king of Wu said bitterly, “The realm is one family; if he died in Chang’an, let him be buried in Chang’an—why send him back here?”
translations.idiomatic: The king of Wu growled, “All under heaven are one clan; if he died in Chang’an he should lie in Chang’an—why drag him back to Wu?”

sentences.zh: 復遣喪之長安葬。

translations.literal: They sent the remains back to Chang’an for burial after all.
translations.idiomatic: The remains were forwarded to Chang’an for interment.

sentences.zh: 吳王由是怨望，稍失藩臣禮，稱疾不朝。

translations.literal: From that day Liu Bi nursed a grudge. He gradually neglected the courtesies owed a vassal, feigned illness, and stopped coming to court.
translations.idiomatic: From then on Liu Bi harbored resentment, slackened the ritual due from a vassal, pleaded sickness, and stayed away from audience.

sentences.zh: 京師知其以子故，驗問實不病，諸吳使來，輒繫責治之。

translations.literal: The capital knew it was about his son; inquiries proved he was not sick, so whenever envoys arrived from Wu they were detained and punished.
translations.idiomatic: The court understood the slight was about his son; investigations showed he was not ill, and Wu envoys were routinely seized and chastised.

sentences.zh: 吳王恐，所謀滋甚。

translations.literal: The king grew afraid, and his schemes grew more desperate.
translations.idiomatic: Fear drove him, and his plotting grew wilder.

sentences.zh: 及後使人為秋請，上復責問吳使者。

translations.literal: Later, when his men came to ask leave for the autumn audience, the emperor again rebuked Wu’s messengers.
translations.idiomatic: When he later sent men to claim the autumn exemption from court, the emperor again dressed down Wu’s envoys.

sentences.zh: 使者曰：「察見淵中魚，不祥。

translations.literal: The envoy replied, “It is ill luck to spy every fish in a clear pool.
translations.idiomatic: The messenger answered, “Folk wisdom says that to see to the bottom of a pool is unlucky.

sentences.zh: 今吳王始詐疾，反覺，見責急，愈益閉，恐上誅之，計乃無聊。

translations.literal: “Our king first pretended sickness; once exposed, he was pressed so hard that he shut himself away all the more, dreading execution and seeing no honorable course left.”
translations.idiomatic: “Our king feigned illness; when that was seen through and blame piled on, he withdrew further, fearing the axe and finding no way out.”

sentences.zh: 唯上與更始。」

translations.literal: “We beg Your Majesty to give him a fresh start.”
translations.idiomatic: “We beg you to grant him a new beginning.”

sentences.zh: 於是天子皆赦吳使者歸之，而賜吳王几杖，老，不朝。

translations.literal: The emperor pardoned Wu’s envoys and sent them home, then bestowed on the king of Wu an arm-rest and staff as tokens of age, exempting him from court attendance.
translations.idiomatic: The Son of Heaven pardoned the envoys and sent them back, then gave the king of Wu a folding stool and staff in token of old age, freeing him from court calls.

sentences.zh: 吳得釋，其謀亦益解。

translations.literal: With that pressure lifted, the king’s rebellious planning also eased.
translations.idiomatic: Wu breathed again, and the conspiracy cooled.

sentences.zh: 然其居國以銅鹽故，百姓無賦。

translations.literal: Still, inside his kingdom the profits of coinage and salt meant that ordinary households paid no grain tax.
translations.idiomatic: Within his borders copper and salt still paid the bills, so the commoners owed no land tax.

sentences.zh: 卒踐更，輒予平賈。

translations.literal: Whenever commoners owed their annual labor service, he paid them the standard wage in cash.
translations.idiomatic: When militia duty fell due, he paid them the fair market rate.

sentences.zh: 歲時存問茂材，賞賜閭里。

translations.literal: At the festivals he visited local talent and showered gifts on whole villages.
translations.idiomatic: At New Year and other seasons he looked in on promising men and scattered largesse through the neighborhoods.

sentences.zh: 它郡國吏欲來捕亡人者，頌共禁不與。

translations.literal: If officers from other jurisdictions came to arrest fugitives, the Wu authorities shielded them and refused cooperation.
translations.idiomatic: If other commanderies sent bailiffs for runaways, Wu colluded to hide them.

sentences.zh: 如此者三十餘年，以故能使其眾。

translations.literal: For more than three decades he managed his people in this way, which is why he could command their loyalty.
translations.idiomatic: He did this for over thirty years, and so won the hearts of his people.

sentences.zh: 漢廷臣方議削吳，吳王恐削地無已，因欲發謀舉事。

translations.literal: When Han ministers debated stripping Wu of land, Liu Bi feared endless confiscations and resolved to act on his conspiracy.
translations.idiomatic: As the Han court debated carving up Wu, the king dreaded endless reductions and meant to launch his plot.

sentences.zh: 念諸侯無足與計者，聞膠西王勇，好兵，諸侯皆畏憚之，於是乃使中大夫應高口說膠西王曰：「吳王不肖，有夙夜之憂，不敢自外，使使臣諭其愚心。」

translations.literal: He saw no peer among the other princes with whom to conspire. Learning that the king of Jiaoxi was bold and loved war, and that the other lords all stood in awe of him, he dispatched Palace Counsellor Ying Gao to sound him out without a written memorial. Ying Gao said on the Wu king’s behalf, “Our unworthy sovereign carries a constant anxiety and cannot treat you as a stranger; he sends me to lay his humble thoughts before you.”
translations.idiomatic: He judged no other feudal lord fit to scheme with, but heard that the king of Jiaoxi was brave and warlike and that the princes all feared him. He therefore sent Palace Counsellor Ying Gao with an oral message: “The king of Wu, unworthy as he is, lies awake nights with worry; he dares not keep you at arm’s length and sends me to voice his foolish heart.”

sentences.zh: 王曰：「何以教之？」

translations.literal: The king asked, “What counsel do you bring?”
translations.idiomatic: The king said, “What would you teach me?”

sentences.zh: 高曰：「今者主上任用邪臣，聽信讒賊，變更律令，侵削諸侯，徵求滋多，誅罰良重，日以益甚。

translations.literal: Gao said, “The throne now rests in the hands of treacherous ministers who spread slander, who endlessly revise statutes, whittle away the princely domains, multiply exactions, and pile cruel punishments upon good men—each day worse than the last.
translations.idiomatic: Ying Gao said, “The sovereign now trusts wicked ministers, heeds slanderers, alters laws and orders, gnaws at the kingdoms, heaps new demands, and punishes with growing severity—worse every day.

sentences.zh: 語有之曰：「舐糠及米。」

translations.literal: There is an old saying: ‘Lick the bran off the grain and you will taste the rice.’
translations.idiomatic: There is a proverb: ‘Lick the chaff and you reach the grain.’

sentences.zh: 吳與膠西，知名諸侯也，一時見察，不得安肆矣。

translations.literal: Wu and Jiaoxi are celebrated kingdoms; the moment the court turns a harsh eye on us, we shall no longer live at ease.”
translations.idiomatic: Wu and Jiaoxi are famous states; once we fall under close scrutiny we can never again live at ease.”

sentences.zh: 吳王身有內疾，不能朝請二十餘年，常患見疑，無以自白，脅肩絫足，猶懼不見釋。

translations.literal: “For more than twenty years our king has pleaded internal ailment and avoided court; he has long dreaded suspicion with no way to clear himself. Even hunched in fear he dreads he may never be spared.”
translations.idiomatic: “The king of Wu has feigned inner sickness and skipped court audiences for twenty years, always fearing mistrust with no way to clear his name. He shrinks and cringes yet still doubts he will be forgiven.”

sentences.zh: 竊聞大王以爵事有過，所聞諸侯削地，罪不至此，此恐不止削地而已。」

translations.literal: “I hear Your Majesty was fined over your noble ranks; stripping commanderies is punishment beyond the offense. This may not end with territory alone.”
translations.idiomatic: “I hear Your Majesty lost rank over the sale of noble titles; the talk of stripping whole commanderies goes beyond the crime—I fear it will not stop at land.”

sentences.zh: 王曰：「有之，子將柰何？」

translations.literal: The king replied, “It is so. What would you have me do?”
translations.idiomatic: The king said, “That is true. What then?”

sentences.zh: 高曰：「同惡相助，同好相留，同情相求，同欲相趨，同利相死。

translations.literal: “Shared hatred brings mutual aid, shared likes mutual shelter, shared sentiment mutual pursuit, shared appetite mutual rush, shared gain brings men to die for one another.”
translations.idiomatic: “Those who hate the same foe help one another; those who love the same ends keep one another; those who feel the same seek one another; those who desire the same flock together; those who profit together die for one another.”

sentences.zh: 今吳王自以與大王同憂，願因時循理，棄軀以除患於天下，意亦可乎？」

translations.literal: “Now the King of Wu believes he shares your peril and wishes to seize the moment in good order—to hazard himself and rid the realm of this scourge. Would that suit your mind?”
translations.idiomatic: “The king of Wu thinks he shares your danger and wishes to seize the hour in good order—to stake his life and wipe this evil from the realm. Would that suit you?”

sentences.zh: 膠西王瞿然駭曰：「寡人何敢如是？

translations.literal: The King of Jiaoxi started in alarm: “How could I presume to anything of the kind?
translations.idiomatic: The king of Jiaoxi started, aghast: “How should I dare such a thing?

sentences.zh: 主上雖急，固有死耳，安得不事？」

translations.literal: “The Son of Heaven may press me hard, but at worst there is always death; how could I refuse to serve him?”
translations.idiomatic: “The sovereign may drive me hard, but there is still only death at the end—how could I fail to serve him?”

sentences.zh: 高曰：「御史大夫朝錯熒惑天子，侵奪諸侯，蔽忠塞賢，朝廷疾怨，諸侯皆有背叛之意，人事極矣。

translations.literal: Gao answered, “Grandee Secretary Chao Cuo dazzles the sovereign, carves up the princes, silences loyal men and worthy advisors, until both court and kingdoms seethe with resentment and every prince harbors thoughts of revolt. Human patience is stretched to the limit.
translations.idiomatic: Ying Gao said, “Grandee Secretary Chao Cuo dazzles the Son of Heaven, strips the feudatories, blocks the loyal and worthy, so that the court boils with anger and every prince nurses rebellion. The crisis is complete.

sentences.zh: 彗星出，蝗蟲起，此萬世一時，而愁勞，聖人所以起也。

translations.literal: Comets streak the sky and locust clouds rise—such a conjunction comes once in an age and draws forth weary sages.”
translations.idiomatic: Comets appear and locusts swarm—such a moment comes once in ten thousand generations, and it is out of such grief and toil that sages rise.”

sentences.zh: 吳王內以朝錯為誅，外從大王後車，方洋天下，所向者降，所指者下，莫敢不服。

translations.literal: “The King of Wu will take the execution of Chao Cuo as his opening demand in the heartland; in the field he would follow at Your Majesty’s chariot-wheel, roaming the realm so that every city that faces him yields and every army he points at kneels. Who would dare refuse?”
translations.idiomatic: “The king of Wu will move first against Chao Cuo under the banner of clearing the court; abroad he would march in your wake, sweeping the realm until every place you face surrenders and every host you point at falls—none would dare resist.”

sentences.zh: 大王誠幸而許之一言，則吳王率楚王略函谷關，守滎陽敖倉之粟，距漢兵，治次舍，須大王。

translations.literal: “If Your Majesty will but pledge a single word, the King of Wu will march with the King of Chu to seize Hangu Pass, hold the grain of Ao Granary at Xingyang, block the imperial armies, prepare encampments, and await your arrival.”
translations.idiomatic: “If you will but nod once, the king of Wu will lead the king of Chu to seize Hangu Pass, guard the grain at Ao Granary by Xingyang, hold the Han armies at bay, pitch camp, and wait for you.”

sentences.zh: 大王幸而臨之，則天下可并，兩主分割，不亦可乎？」

translations.literal: “If you then ride to join him, the empire can be seized and the two of you might divide it between yourselves—would that not serve?”
translations.idiomatic: “If you then join him in person, the world may be shared and the two thrones split—would that not be well?”

sentences.zh: 王曰：「善。」

translations.literal: “Good!” said the king.
translations.idiomatic: The king said, “Good.”

sentences.zh: 歸報吳王，猶恐其不果，乃身自為使者，至膠西面約之。

translations.literal: Ying Gao returned his report, yet the Wu king still doubted his commitment and went in person as envoy to Jiaoxi to conclude the pact face to face.
translations.idiomatic: Ying Gao reported back, but the king of Wu still feared he might waver and traveled himself to Jiaoxi to seal the bargain mouth to mouth.

sentences.zh: 膠西群臣或聞王謀，諫曰：「諸侯地不能為漢十二，為叛逆以憂太后，非計也。

translations.literal: Some of the King of Jiaoxi’s ministers overheard the plot and remonstrated: “The princes together hold less than one-twelfth of the Han realm; to rise in mutiny would only distress our queen mother—it is no sound stratagem.
translations.idiomatic: Some of the king of Jiaoxi’s ministers heard the plan and urged him: “The feudal lands are not one-twelfth of the Han; to rebel would only grieve the empress dowager—it is no policy.

sentences.zh: 今承一帝，尚云不易，假令事成，兩主分爭，患乃益生。」

translations.literal: “Even now, under a single emperor, rule is hard enough; were the enterprise to succeed and two thrones afterward quarrel, calamity would only multiply.”
translations.idiomatic: “We already serve one Son of Heaven and call it difficult; if you succeeded and two lords then fell out, disaster would only grow.”

sentences.zh: 王不聽，遂發使約齊、菑川、膠東、濟南，皆許諾。

translations.literal: The king would not listen. Envoys were dispatched to Qi, Zichuan, Jiaodong, and Jinan; every party pledged assent.
translations.idiomatic: He would not heed them. He sent envoys to covenant with Qi, Zichuan, Jiaodong, and Jinan, and each agreed.

sentences.zh: 諸侯既新削罰，震恐，多怨錯。

translations.literal: The princes, fresh from punitive confiscations, trembled with fear and for the most part blamed Chao Cuo.
translations.idiomatic: The princes, newly shorn by edict, were shaken and for the most part hated Chao Cuo.

sentences.zh: 乃削吳會稽、豫章郡書至，則吳王先起兵，誅漢吏二千石以下。

translations.literal: When the edict ordering Wu to surrender the commanderies of Kuaiji and Yuzhang reached him, the King of Wu was first to rise in arms and put every Han official of two-thousand-bushel rank and below to the sword.
translations.idiomatic: When the rescript arrived stripping Wu of Kuaiji and Yuzhang, the king of Wu was the first to raise troops and execute every Han officer from two-thousand-bushel rank downward.

sentences.zh: 膠西、膠東、菑川、濟南、楚、趙亦皆反，發兵西。

translations.literal: Jiaoxi, Jiaodong, Zichuan, Jinan, Chu, and Zhao likewise rebelled and raised troops westward.
translations.idiomatic: Jiaoxi, Jiaodong, Zichuan, Jinan, Chu, and Zhao rose as well, each sending armies toward the west.

sentences.zh: 齊王後悔，背約城守。

translations.literal: The King of Qi repented, broke the covenant, and defended his city.
translations.idiomatic: The king of Qi had second thoughts, abandoned the pact, and shut himself behind his walls.

sentences.zh: 濟北王城壞未完，其郎中令劫守王，不得發兵。

translations.literal: The King of Jinbei's walls were damaged and not yet finished; his palace gentleman commandant seized and held the king, so he could not mobilize.
translations.idiomatic: Jinbei’s ramparts were still in ruins; his gentleman of the palace took him hostage, and no troops could be raised.

sentences.zh: 膠西王、膠東王為渠率，與菑川、濟南共攻圍臨菑。

translations.literal: The kings of Jiaoxi and Jiaodong served as commanders-in-chief and, with Zichuan and Jinan together, attacked and besieged Linzi.
translations.idiomatic: The kings of Jiaoxi and Jiaodong led the host, and with Zichuan and Jinan jointly invested Linzi.

sentences.zh: 趙王遂亦陰使匈奴與連兵。

translations.literal: King Zhao Sui also secretly sent to the Xiongnu to link armies.
translations.idiomatic: King Zhao Sui likewise sent secret word to the Xiongnu to join forces.

sentences.zh: 七國之發也，吳王悉其士卒，下令國中曰：「寡人年六十二，身自將。

translations.literal: When the seven states rose, the King of Wu called up every able soldier and issued an order throughout the kingdom: “This old man is sixty-two and will lead in person.”
translations.idiomatic: As the seven kingdoms rose, Liu Bi mustered his entire host and proclaimed: “I am sixty-two and will take the field myself.”

sentences.zh: 少子年十四，亦為士卒先。

translations.literal: My youngest son is fourteen and shall march at the head of the ranks.”
translations.idiomatic: “My youngest boy is fourteen; he too will stand in the front rank.”

sentences.zh: 諸年上與寡人同，下與少子等，皆發。」

translations.literal: All those whose years lie between mine and my youngest son's shall be mobilized.”
translations.idiomatic: “Every man from his age down to my youngest son’s shall be called up.”

sentences.zh: 二十餘萬人。

translations.literal: More than two hundred thousand men.
translations.idiomatic: They mustered over two hundred thousand fighting men.

sentences.zh: 南使閩、東越，閩、東越亦發兵從。

translations.literal: Southward he sent to Min and Eastern Yue; Min and Eastern Yue also raised troops to follow.
translations.idiomatic: He sent south to Min and Eastern Yue, and those states likewise mobilized to march with him.

sentences.zh: 孝景前三年正月甲子，初起兵於廣陵。

translations.literal: On the jiǎzǐ day of the first month in the third year of Emperor Xiaojing, he first raised troops at Guangling.
translations.idiomatic: On jiǎzǐ in the first month of Xiaojing’s third year the rebellion opened at Guangling.

sentences.zh: 西涉淮，因并楚兵。

translations.literal: Westward he crossed the Huai and absorbed the Chu army.
translations.idiomatic: He crossed the Huai westward and merged his columns with Chu’s host.

sentences.zh: 發使遺諸侯書曰：「吳王劉濞敬問膠西王、膠東王、菑川王、濟南王、趙王、楚王、淮南王、衡山王、廬江王、故長沙王子：幸教！

translations.literal: He sent envoys bearing a letter to the princes: “Liu Bi, King of Wu, respectfully inquires of the kings of Jiaoxi, Jiaodong, Zichuan, Jinan, Zhao, Chu, Huainan, Hengshan, Lujiang, and the son of the former King of Changsha: I beg your counsel!”
translations.idiomatic: His messengers carried a manifesto: “The king of Wu Liu Bi greets the kings of Jiaoxi, Jiaodong, Zichuan, Jinan, Zhao, Chu, Huainan, Hengshan, Lujiang, and the heir of old Changsha—may you instruct me!”

sentences.zh: 以漢有賊臣錯，無功天下，侵奪諸侯之地，使吏劾繫訊治，以侵辱之為故，不以諸侯人君禮遇劉氏骨肉，絕先帝功臣，進任姦人，誑亂天下，欲危社稷。

translations.literal: Because Han harbors the traitorous minister Chao Cuo, who has won no merit for the realm yet seizes the princes' lands, sends officers to impeach, bind, and interrogate them, and makes deliberate humiliation his constant practice—he does not treat the Liu flesh and blood with the courtesy due princes, cuts off the founding emperor's meritorious servants, promotes villains, throws the realm into confusion, and would endanger the altars of soil and grain.
translations.idiomatic: Han is ridden by the traitor Chao Cuo, a man without service to the empire who gnaws at princely fiefs, hounds nobles with indictments and chains, insults them by design, dishonors the Liu kin, casts aside Gaozu’s old ministers, elevates scoundrels, and would overturn the state.

sentences.zh: 陛下多病志逸，不能省察。

translations.literal: The sovereign is often ill in mind and body and cannot look into these matters.
translations.idiomatic: The Son of Heaven is sick in body and distracted in will; he cannot see through the plot.

sentences.zh: 欲舉兵誅之，謹聞教。

translations.literal: We intend to raise arms to execute him and respectfully await your teaching.”
translations.idiomatic: We mean to take up arms to put Cuo to the sword and await your orders.”

sentences.zh: 敝國雖狹，地方三千里；

translations.literal: Our poor state though narrow still covers three thousand li of ground;
translations.idiomatic: “Wu is small, yet its territory runs three thousand li;

sentences.zh: 人民雖少，精兵可具五十萬。

translations.literal: our people though few can still equip five hundred thousand picked troops.
translations.idiomatic: its people are not numerous, yet half a million sharp soldiers can be armed.”

sentences.zh: 寡人素事南越三十餘年，其王諸君皆不辭分其兵以隨寡人，又可得三十萬。

translations.literal: For more than thirty years I have cultivated ties with Southern Yue; its kings and lords will not refuse to divide their forces and follow me—I can gain another three hundred thousand.
translations.idiomatic: I have dealt with Southern Yue for thirty years; its rulers will gladly detach troops to march with me and add another three hundred thousand blades.”

sentences.zh: 寡人雖不肖，願以身從諸王。

translations.literal: Though I am unworthy, I would follow the kings in person.”
translations.idiomatic: “Unworthy as I am, I will take the field at your side.”

sentences.zh: 南越直長沙者，因王子定長沙以北，西走蜀、漢中。

translations.literal: What of Southern Yue borders Changsha shall, through the princes' sons, secure the lands north of Changsha and strike west into Shu and Hanzhong.
translations.idiomatic: Where Southern Yue touches Changsha, let the princes’ sons secure everything north of Changsha and drive west into Shu and Hanzhong.”

sentences.zh: 告越、楚王、淮南三王，與寡人西面；

translations.literal: Inform Yue, the King of Chu, and the three Huainan kings to march west with me;
translations.idiomatic: Tell Yue, the king of Chu, and the three Huainan kings to wheel west with me;

sentences.zh: 齊諸王與趙王定河間、河內，或入臨晉關，或與寡人會雒陽；

translations.literal: the kings of Qi with the King of Zhao shall secure Hejian and Henei, either enter Linjin Pass or rendezvous with me at Luoyang;
translations.idiomatic: let the Qi princes and the king of Zhao take Hejian and Henei—some to force Linjin Pass, others to meet me at Luoyang;

sentences.zh: 燕王、趙王故與胡王有約，燕王北定代、雲中，轉胡眾入蕭關，走長安，匡正天下，以安高廟。

translations.literal: the Kings of Yan and Zhao formerly had covenants with the Hu king—the King of Yan shall secure north Dai and Yunzhong, wheel the Hu hosts through Xiaoguan toward Chang'an, rectify the realm, and settle the Founder Temple.
translations.idiomatic: Yan and Zhao long ago pledged the steppe lords: Yan shall pacify Dai and Yunzhong in the north, swing the nomad horse through Xiaoguan toward Chang’an, set the realm right, and guard Gaozu’s shrine.”

sentences.zh: 願王勉之。

translations.literal: I pray the kings will exert themselves.”
translations.idiomatic: “I urge every king to bend his utmost.”

sentences.zh: 楚元王子、淮南三王或不沐洗十餘年，怨入骨髓，欲壹有所出久矣，寡人未得諸王之意，未敢聽。

translations.literal: The sons of King Chu Yuan and the three Huainan kings have perhaps not bathed or washed for ten years; hatred runs to their marrow; they have long wished for a single chance to strike—I have not yet taken the princes' measure and dare not lightly heed them.
translations.idiomatic: The Chu Yuan princes and the three Huainan lines have nursed grudges for a decade without rest; I have not sounded their temper and dare not commit until I know their minds.”

sentences.zh: 今諸王苟能存亡繼絕，振弱伐暴，以安劉氏，社稷所願也。

translations.literal: Now if the princes can preserve the doomed and continue extinct lines, raise the weak and punish the violent, thereby to secure the house of Liu, that is what the altars would wish.
translations.idiomatic: If you can succor the fallen, restore broken lines, champion the weak, and chastise the cruel for the sake of the Liu house, the spirits of soil and grain will bless you.”

sentences.zh: 吳國雖貧，寡人節衣食用，積金錢，脩兵革，聚糧食，夜以繼日，三十餘年矣。

translations.literal: Wu though poor—for thirty years I have stinted my dress and food, hoarded gold and cash, readied armor and weapons, gathered grain, worked night into day.
translations.idiomatic: Wu is poor, yet thirty years I have pinched my table and wardrobe, piled gold, forged arms, stored grain, and toiled from dusk to dawn.”

sentences.zh: 凡皆為此，願諸王勉之。

translations.literal: All of it has been for this; I pray the kings will exert themselves.”
translations.idiomatic: “Everything I have done aims at this hour—again, I beg you to strive.”

sentences.zh: 能斬捕大將者，賜金五千斤，封萬戶；

translations.literal: Whoever beheads or captures a grand general shall receive five thousand catties of gold and a fief of ten thousand households;
translations.idiomatic: Strike down an enemy commander-in-chief and you take five thousand catties of gold and a ten-thousand-household marquisate;

sentences.zh: 列將，三千斤，封五千戶；

translations.literal: a column commander, three thousand catties and a fief of five thousand households;
translations.idiomatic: a corps commander earns three thousand catties and five thousand households;

sentences.zh: 裨將，二千斤，封二千戶；

translations.literal: a deputy commander, two thousand catties and a fief of two thousand households;
translations.idiomatic: a lieutenant-general two thousand catties and two thousand households;

sentences.zh: 二千石，千斤，封千戶：皆為列侯。

translations.literal: an official of two thousand piculs, one thousand catties and a fief of one thousand households—all shall be full marquises.
translations.idiomatic: any two-thousand-picul officer brings a thousand catties and a thousand-household fief—all such men become full marquises.”

sentences.zh: 其以軍若城邑降者，卒萬人，邑萬戶，如得大將；

translations.literal: Those who surrender with an army or a walled city: for ten thousand soldiers and a town of ten thousand households, reward as for capturing a grand general;
translations.idiomatic: Surrender a host or a city: ten thousand soldiers and ten thousand households pay like taking a commander-in-chief;

sentences.zh: 人戶五千，如得列將；

translations.literal: five thousand households as for capturing a column commander;
translations.idiomatic: five thousand households rates a corps commander’s prize;

sentences.zh: 人戶三千，如得裨將；

translations.literal: three thousand households as for capturing a deputy commander;
translations.idiomatic: three thousand households a lieutenant-general’s;

sentences.zh: 人戶千，如得二千石；

translations.literal: one thousand households as for capturing an official of two thousand piculs;
translations.idiomatic: one thousand households matches a two-thousand-picul captive;

sentences.zh: 其小吏皆以差次受爵金。

translations.literal: lesser officers shall receive rank and gold in graduated order.
translations.idiomatic: junior ranks receive titles and gold by graded steps.”

sentences.zh: 它封賜皆倍軍法。

translations.literal: All other enfeoffments and gifts shall double the military ordinance scale.
translations.idiomatic: Every other bounty runs at twice the usual army scale.”

sentences.zh: 其有故爵邑者，更益勿因。

translations.literal: Those who already hold noble rank and fiefs shall receive additional grants without stacking on the old.
translations.idiomatic: Men who already hold rank and fief gain fresh reward without canceling the old.”

sentences.zh: 願諸王明以令士大夫，不敢欺也。

translations.literal: I pray the kings will openly publish these terms to their officers and men—I will not deceive them.”
translations.idiomatic: Publish this plainly to your officers—I will not cheat them.”

sentences.zh: 寡人金錢在天下者往往而有，非必取於吳，諸王日夜用之不能盡。

translations.literal: My gold and cash are scattered throughout the realm and need not be drawn only from Wu; the kings may spend them day and night without exhausting them.
translations.idiomatic: My treasure lies everywhere under heaven, not only in Wu; spend it night and day—you cannot empty it.”

sentences.zh: 有當賜者告寡人，寡人且往遺之。

translations.literal: When any are due reward, inform me and I will go in person to bestow it.
translations.idiomatic: Name the worthy and I will ride to hand them their share.”

sentences.zh: 敬以聞。」

translations.literal: Respectfully submitted for your hearing.”
translations.idiomatic: So I report, with respect.”

sentences.zh: 七國反書聞，天子乃遣太尉條侯周亞夫將三十六將軍往擊吳楚；

translations.literal: When word of the seven states' rebellion reached the throne, the Son of Heaven dispatched Grand Commandant Marquis of Tiaohou Zhou Yafu with thirty-six generals to strike Wu and Chu;
translations.idiomatic: News of the seven kingdoms’ revolt reached court; the emperor sent Grand Commandant Zhou Yafu of Tiaohou with thirty-six generals against Wu and Chu;

sentences.zh: 遣曲周侯酈寄擊趙，將軍欒布擊齊，大將軍竇嬰屯滎陽監齊趙兵。

translations.literal: he sent Marquis of Qüzhou Li Ji against Zhao and General Luan Bu against Qi; Grand General Dou Ying encamped at Xingyang to oversee the Qi and Zhao armies.
translations.idiomatic: he ordered Li Ji of Qüzhou to hit Zhao, Luan Bu to hit Qi, and stationed Grand General Dou Ying at Xingyang to coordinate the northern fronts.

sentences.zh: 條侯將乘六乘傳，會兵滎陽。

translations.literal: Lord Tiaohou would ride six relay post carriages and assemble troops at Xingyang.
translations.idiomatic: Zhou Yafu raced six post-horses to rally the host at Xingyang.

sentences.zh: 至雒陽，見劇孟，喜曰：「

translations.literal: Reaching Luoyang he saw Ju Meng and said with pleasure:
translations.idiomatic: At Luoyang he met Ju Meng and exclaimed:

sentences.zh: 七國反，吾乘傳至此，不自意全。

translations.literal: The seven states have rebelled; I rode the relay to this place and did not expect to arrive unharmed.
translations.idiomatic: Seven kingdoms in arms—I rode the relays here and hardly hoped to come through intact.

sentences.zh: 又以為諸侯已得劇孟。

translations.literal: I had also thought the princes had already secured Ju Meng.
translations.idiomatic: I feared the rebels had already bought Ju Meng.

sentences.zh: 孟今無動，吾據滎陽，滎陽以東無足憂者。」

translations.literal: Now that Meng has not stirred, I hold Xingyang, and east of Xingyang there is nothing to worry about.”
translations.idiomatic: Since he has not moved, Xingyang is mine, and nothing east of it should frighten us.”

sentences.zh: 至淮陽，問故父絳侯客鄧都尉曰：「策安出？」

translations.literal: At Huaiyang he questioned his late father's client, Commandant Deng of the Marquis of Jiang, saying, “What strategy should we adopt?”
translations.idiomatic: In Huaiyang he asked Deng, the Jiang marquis’s old retainer, now commandant: “What is your counsel?”

sentences.zh: 客曰：「吳楚兵銳甚，難與爭鋒。

translations.literal: The client replied, “The Wu and Chu soldiers are very keen-edged; it will be hard to contend with their spear-points.
translations.idiomatic: Deng said: “Wu and Chu are razor-sharp; do not meet their point.

sentences.zh: 楚兵輕，不能久。

translations.literal: Chu troops are light and cannot last long.
translations.idiomatic: Chu’s men are light and cannot endure a long war.

sentences.zh: 方今為將軍計，莫若引兵東北壁昌邑，以梁委吳，吳必盡銳攻之。

translations.literal: For the general's present purpose, nothing is better than leading the army northeast to fortify Changyi, throw Liang to Wu, and Wu will surely commit all its keen edge against it.
translations.idiomatic: Better march northeast, dig in at Changyi, feed Liang to Wu, and let Wu spend its full fury there.

sentences.zh: 將軍深溝高壘，使輕兵絕淮泗口，塞吳饟道。

translations.literal: The general shall dig deep moats and raise high ramparts, send light troops to cut the Huai and Si mouths, and block Wu's supply roads.
translations.idiomatic: Hold behind deep ditches and high walls, send skirmishers to choke the Huai–Si junction, and sever Wu’s grain lines.

sentences.zh: 使吳、梁相敝而糧食竭，乃以全制其極，破吳必矣。」

translations.literal: Let Wu and Liang exhaust each other until grain is gone; then with the full intact force you may pin them at their limit—Wu will certainly be broken.”
translations.idiomatic: When Wu and Liang have worn each other out and the wagons are empty, strike with fresh strength—Wu must fall.”

sentences.zh: 條侯曰：「善。」

translations.literal: Lord Tiaohou said, “Good.”
translations.idiomatic: Zhou Yafu said, “Agreed.”

sentences.zh: 從其策，遂堅壁昌邑南，輕兵絕吳饟道。

translations.literal: He followed this plan, therefore held fast in fortifications south of Changyi, and sent light troops to cut Wu's supply lines.
translations.idiomatic: He dug in south of Changyi and sent light horse to strangle Wu’s convoys.

sentences.zh: 吳王之初發也，吳臣田祿伯為大將軍。

translations.literal: When the King of Wu first rose, Wu minister Tian Lubo served as grand general.
translations.idiomatic: At the outbreak Liu Bi made his minister Tian Lubo grand general.

sentences.zh: 田祿伯曰：「兵屯聚而西，無它奇道，難以立功。

translations.literal: Tian Lubo said, “The army masses and marches west—there is no other clever path; it will be hard to win merit.
translations.idiomatic: Lubo argued: “A single mass marching west has no surprise in it; glory will be hard to win.

sentences.zh: 臣願得五萬人，別循江淮而上，收淮南、長沙，入武關，與大王會，此亦一奇也。」

translations.literal: I beg fifty thousand men to strike separately up the Yangzi and Huai, seize Huainan and Changsha, enter Wu Pass, and rendezvous with Your Majesty—that would also be one stroke of surprise.”
translations.idiomatic: Give me fifty thousand to run the Yangzi and Huai upstream, take Huainan and Changsha, burst through Wu Pass, and meet you—that is a second front.”

sentences.zh: 吳王太子諫曰：「王以反為名，此兵難以藉人，人亦且反王，柰何？

translations.literal: The Wu heir remonstrated: “You rise in the name of revolt—this army cannot be lent to others, or others will revolt against you in turn—what then?
translations.idiomatic: The crown prince objected: “We march under the banner of rebellion; you cannot hand troops to another man or he may turn them on you—what then?

sentences.zh: 且擅兵而別，多它利害，徒自損耳。」

translations.literal: Moreover, to split the command of troops separately invites many other gains and harms; you would only harm yourself.”
translations.idiomatic: Splitting the army breeds endless risk—you would only wound yourself.”

sentences.zh: 吳王即不許田祿伯。

translations.literal: Thereupon the King of Wu did not grant Tian Lubo's request.
translations.idiomatic: The king refused Lubo’s plan.

sentences.zh: 吳少將桓將軍說王曰：「吳多步兵，步兵利險；

translations.literal: Young General Huan of Wu urged the king, saying, “Wu has many infantry; infantry profit from rough ground;
translations.idiomatic: Young General Huan urged him: “Wu fields foot soldiers; foot soldiers love broken ground;

sentences.zh: 漢多車騎，車騎利平地。

translations.literal: Han has many chariots and cavalry; chariots and cavalry profit from open plains.
translations.idiomatic: Han fields chariots and horse; chariots and horse love the flat.

sentences.zh: 願大王所過城不下，直去，疾西據雒陽武庫，食敖倉粟，阻山河之險以令諸侯，雖無入關，天下固已定矣。

translations.literal: I pray Your Majesty, wherever you pass a city that does not submit, do not linger—press straight on, speed west to seize the Luoyang armory, eat the grain of Ao Granary, block the barrier strength of mountains and rivers to command the princes—even without entering the passes the realm would already be settled.
translations.idiomatic: Pass every unwalled town without stopping, race to Luoyang’s arsenal, feed on Ao Granary, straddle the mountain rivers, and cow the lords—you need not enter the passes to own the realm.

sentences.zh: 大王徐行，留下城邑，漢軍車騎至，馳入梁楚之郊，事敗矣。」

translations.literal: If Your Majesty moves slowly, leaving walled towns behind, Han's chariots and cavalry arrive and gallop into the borderlands of Liang and Chu—the affair is lost.”
translations.idiomatic: Linger to siege cities and Han’s cavalry will pour into Liang and Chu’s border—you are finished.”

sentences.zh: 吳王問吳老將，老將曰：「此年少椎鋒可耳，安知大慮！」

translations.literal: The King of Wu questioned Wu's senior generals; they said, “This young man can rush the spear-point—that is all; how would he know grand strategy!”
translations.idiomatic: Liu Bi asked the greybeards; they sneered, “Boys can charge the van—what do they know of grand design?”

sentences.zh: 於是王不用桓將軍計。

translations.literal: Therefore the king did not employ General Huan's plan.
translations.idiomatic: So Huan’s advice was cast aside.

sentences.zh: 王專并將其兵，未度淮，諸賓客皆得為將、校尉、行間候、司馬，獨周丘不用。

translations.literal: The king personally commanded all his troops. Before he had crossed the Huai, every guest-advisor had received appointment as general, colonel, marching scout, or major—only Zhou Qiu was not used.
translations.idiomatic: The king held sole command. Before the Huai every client had become general, colonel, scout, or major—only Zhou Qiu was left out.

sentences.zh: 周丘者，下邳人，亡命吳，酤酒無行，王薄之，不任。

translations.literal: Zhou Qiu was a native of Xiapi, a fugitive in Wu, a wine-seller of bad conduct; the king despised him and would not employ him.
translations.idiomatic: Zhou Qiu of Xiapi was a runaway in Wu, a tavern rogue the king scorned and would not use.

sentences.zh: 周丘乃上謁，說王曰：「臣以無能，不得待罪行間。

translations.literal: Zhou Qiu thereupon presented himself and said to the king, “Because I lack ability I have not been employed among the ranks.
translations.idiomatic: He forced an audience: “For my faults I have no place in the ranks.

sentences.zh: 臣非敢求有所將也，願請王一漢節，必有以報。」

translations.literal: I do not presume to beg command of troops; I only beg one Han tally from Your Majesty—I will surely have something to repay.”
translations.idiomatic: I do not ask for an army—only one imperial tally; I will repay you.”

sentences.zh: 王乃予之。

translations.literal: The king then gave it to him.
translations.idiomatic: The king handed it over.

sentences.zh: 周丘得節，夜馳入下邳。

translations.literal: When Zhou Qiu received the tally he galloped at night into Xiapi.
translations.idiomatic: Zhou Qiu took the staff and rode into Xiapi by night.

sentences.zh: 下邳時聞吳反，皆城守。

translations.literal: Xiapi had just heard of Wu's rebellion and all defended the walls.
translations.idiomatic: Xiapi had heard of the revolt and barred its gates.

sentences.zh: 至傳舍，召令入戶，使從者以罪斬令。

translations.literal: He reached the relay station, summoned the magistrate inside the gate, and had his followers execute the magistrate on a fabricated charge.
translations.idiomatic: At the post-house he called the magistrate in and had his men cut him down on a trumped charge.

sentences.zh: 遂召昆弟所善豪吏告曰：「吳反兵且至，屠下邳不過食頃。

translations.literal: He then gathered his brothers' trusted bold officers and told them, “Wu rebel troops will soon arrive; they will sack Xiapi in less than the time of a meal.
translations.idiomatic: He rallied bold kinsmen: “Wu’s host is coming; they will level Xiapi before a meal cools.

sentences.zh: 今先下，家室必完，能者封侯至矣。」

translations.literal: If we yield first our households will certainly be safe, and for the capable there are marquisates to come.”
translations.idiomatic: Yield now and your families live; the able will win marquisates.”

sentences.zh: 出乃相告，下邳皆下。

translations.literal: They went out and spread the word; all Xiapi submitted.
translations.idiomatic: Word ran through the streets and the city opened.

sentences.zh: 周丘一夜得三萬人，使人報吳王，遂將其兵北略城邑。

translations.literal: In one night Zhou Qiu raised thirty thousand men, sent word to the King of Wu, then led this army north to seize towns.
translations.idiomatic: In one night he raised thirty thousand, reported to the king, and swept north taking towns.

sentences.zh: 比至城陽，兵十餘萬，破城陽中尉軍。

translations.literal: By the time he reached Chengyang his force exceeded one hundred thousand and he broke the army of the central commandant of Chengyang.
translations.idiomatic: By Chengyang he had a hundred thousand and shattered the commandant’s corps.

sentences.zh: 聞吳王敗走，自度無與共成功，即引兵歸下邳。

translations.literal: Hearing that the King of Wu had been defeated and fled, he judged there was none with whom to share success, and at once led his troops back toward Xiapi.
translations.idiomatic: When he learned Liu Bi had fled, he saw no partner left for victory and wheeled back toward Xiapi.

sentences.zh: 未至，癰發背死。

translations.literal: Before he arrived a carbuncle broke out on his back and he died.
translations.idiomatic: A back-boil killed him before he arrived.

sentences.zh: 二月，吳王兵既破，敗走，於是天子制詔將軍：「蓋聞為善者天報以福，為非者天報以殃。

translations.literal: In the second month the King of Wu's army had already been broken and fled in defeat; the Son of Heaven then issued an edict to the generals: “We have heard that Heaven rewards the good with blessing and the wicked with calamity.
translations.idiomatic: In the second month Wu’s army was shattered; the emperor told his generals: “Heaven crowns virtue with fortune and strikes evil with woe.

sentences.zh: 高皇帝親垂功德，建立諸侯，幽王、悼惠王絕無後，孝文皇帝哀憐加惠，王幽王子遂，悼惠王子卬等，令奉其先王宗廟，為漢藩國，德配天地，明並日月。

translations.literal: The August Emperor Gaozu personally left merit and virtue, establishing the princes; Kings You of Zhao and Daohui of Qi had no posterity; Emperor Xiaowen took pity and added grace, enfeoffing King You's son Sui and Daohui's son Ang and others, ordering them to maintain their ancestors' temples as Han's outer bulwarks—virtue matching Heaven and Earth, brightness joining sun and moon.
translations.idiomatic: Gaozu set up the kingdoms; when You of Zhao and Daohui of Qi died without heirs, Xiaowen raised Sui, Ang, and the rest to tend their shrines as Han’s shield—his kindness spanned heaven and earth and shone like the luminaries.

sentences.zh: 而吳王濞背德反義，誘受天下亡命罪人，亂天下幣，稱疾不朝二十餘年。

translations.literal: Yet the King of Wu Liu Bi turned his back on virtue and violated righteousness, enticed and harbored fugitive criminals from the realm, threw the realm's currency into confusion, and for twenty years claimed illness and did not attend court.
translations.idiomatic: Yet Liu Bi spurned right, drew runaways, debased the coinage, and feigned sickness twenty years at court.

sentences.zh: 有司數請濞罪，孝文皇帝寬之，欲其改行為善。

translations.literal: The authorities repeatedly requested Bi's punishment; Emperor Xiaowen was lenient and wished him to change his conduct for the good.
translations.idiomatic: Officials begged his head; Wendi spared him, hoping he would mend his ways.

sentences.zh: 今乃與楚王戊、趙王遂、膠西王卬、濟南王辟光、菑川王賢、膠東王雄渠約從謀反，為逆無道，起兵以危宗廟，賊殺大臣及漢使者，迫劫萬民，伐殺無罪，燒殘民家，掘其丘壟，甚為虐暴。

translations.literal: Now he has joined with King Wu of Chu, Liu Wu, King Zhao Sui, King Jiaoxi Ang, King Jinan Pi Guang, King Zichuan Xian, and King Jiaodong Xiongqü in vertical alliance to plot rebellion—unprincipled and lawless—raised troops to endanger the ancestral temples, murdered great ministers and Han envoys, coerced the myriad people, slaughtered the innocent, burned and destroyed commoners' homes, exhumed their tombs—utterly cruel and violent.
translations.idiomatic: Now he leagues with Liu Wu, king of Chu, Liu Sui of Zhao, Liu Ang of Jiaoxi, Pi Guang of Jinan, Liu Xian of Zichuan, and Liu Xiongqü of Jiaodong—lawless traitors who menace the temples, butcher ministers and envoys, dragoon the people, kill the guiltless, burn homes, and open graves—monstrous cruelty.

sentences.zh: 而卬等又重逆無道，燒宗廟，鹵御物，朕甚痛之。

translations.literal: Yet Ang and the others have again been utterly lawless, burned the temples, looted the imperial gear—the pain of this is great in Us.
translations.idiomatic: Ang’s crew have burned the shrines and plundered the palace stores—Our heart is torn.

sentences.zh: 朕素服避正殿，將軍其勸士大夫擊反虜。

translations.literal: We have donned plain dress and avoided the main hall; generals, urge your officers to strike the rebels.
translations.idiomatic: We wear mourning and quit the main hall—generals, drive your men at the traitors.

sentences.zh: 擊反虜者，深入多殺為功，斬首捕虜比三百石以上皆殺，無有所置。

translations.literal: Those who strike rebels shall take deep advance and heavy killing as merit; all rebel leaders beheaded or captured who rank at three hundred piculs or above shall be put to death without sparing.
translations.idiomatic: Pursue deep and kill hard for merit; every rebel chief of three hundred piculs or higher whom you take—execute; grant no quarter.

sentences.zh: 敢有議詔及不如詔者，皆要斬。」

translations.literal: Whoever dares debate the edict or fail to match the edict—waist cut in two.”
translations.idiomatic: Debate the decree or disobey it—death by waist chop.”

sentences.zh: 初，吳王之度淮，與楚王遂西敗棘壁，乘勝而前，銳甚。

translations.literal: Earlier, when the King of Wu crossed the Huai, he and the King of Chu advanced west together, defeated the wall at Ji, pressed forward in victory—very keen.
translations.idiomatic: When Liu Bi crossed the Huai he and the king of Chu drove west, stormed Ji’s rampart, and swept on in high fettle.

sentences.zh: 梁孝王恐，遣將軍擊之，又敗梁兩軍，士卒皆還走。

translations.literal: King Xiaoxiao of Liang was afraid and sent a general against them; they again defeated two Liang armies and the soldiers all ran back.
translations.idiomatic: King Xiao of Liang sent a general; Wu-Chu broke two Liang hosts and the men fled.

sentences.zh: 梁數使使條侯求救，條侯不許。

translations.literal: Liang repeatedly sent envoys begging Lord Tiaohou for rescue; Lord Tiaohou would not consent.
translations.idiomatic: Liang begged Zhou Yafu again and again; he refused.

sentences.zh: 又使使愬條侯於上，上使告條侯救梁，又守便宜不行。

translations.literal: They also sent envoys to denounce Lord Tiaohou to the emperor; the emperor ordered him to save Liang, yet he still held to tactical advantage and did not move.
translations.idiomatic: They complained to the throne; the emperor told him to relieve Liang, yet he clung to his plan and stayed put.

sentences.zh: 梁使韓安國及楚死事相弟張羽為將軍，乃得頗敗吳兵。

translations.literal: Liang then made Han Anguo and Zhang Yu, brother of the minister who died for Chu, generals, and only then were they able to inflict some defeats on the Wu army.
translations.idiomatic: Only when Liang raised Han Anguo and Zhang Yu—brother of Chu’s martyr minister—as generals could they begin to bloody Wu’s advance.

sentences.zh: 吳兵欲西，梁城守，不敢西，即走條侯軍，會下邑。

translations.literal: The Wu army wished to go west, but Liang defended its cities and they dared not advance west; they therefore made for Lord Tiaohou's army and joined battle at Xiayi.
translations.idiomatic: Wu meant to drive west, yet Liang’s walls blocked the way; they dared not push on and veered toward Zhou Yafu’s camp, meeting him below Xiayi.

sentences.zh: 欲戰，條侯壁，不肯戰。

translations.literal: They wished to fight, but Lord Tiaohou held fast behind his ramparts and would not give battle.
translations.idiomatic: They clamored for a fight; Zhou Yafu stayed behind his walls and refused.

sentences.zh: 吳糧絕，卒飢，數挑戰，遂夜奔條侯壁，驚東南。

translations.literal: Wu’s grain was cut off; the soldiers starved. They repeatedly challenged to battle, then by night rushed Lord Tiaohou's ramparts, striking the southeast.
translations.idiomatic: With supplies gone and men famished, Wu taunted day after day, then stormed the southeast corner of Yafu’s lines by night.

sentences.zh: 條侯使備西北，果從西北。

translations.literal: Lord Tiaohou had prepared the northwest; they indeed came from the northwest.
translations.idiomatic: He had stacked his defense on the northwest; the feint failed and the real blow came there.

sentences.zh: 不得入，吳大敗，士卒多飢死叛散。

translations.literal: They could not break in; Wu suffered great defeat, and many soldiers starved to death or deserted.
translations.idiomatic: They never breached the wall. Wu’s host collapsed; thousands starved or melted away.

sentences.zh: 於是吳王乃與其戲下壯士千人夜亡去，度淮走丹徒，保東越。

translations.literal: Thereupon the King of Wu, with a thousand picked warriors of his personal guard, fled by night, crossed the Huai, fled to Dantu, and took refuge with Eastern Yue.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Bi slipped away at the head of a thousand household guards, crossed the Huai, ran to Dantu, and threw himself on Eastern Yue.

sentences.zh: 東越兵可萬餘人，使人收聚亡卒。

translations.literal: Eastern Yue had perhaps more than ten thousand troops and sent men to gather scattered fugitives.
translations.idiomatic: Eastern Yue could field ten thousand blades and began rounding up Wu stragglers.

sentences.zh: 漢使人以利啗東越，東越即紿吳王，吳王出勞軍，使人鏦殺吳王，盛其頭，馳傳以聞。

translations.literal: Han sent men to tempt Eastern Yue with profit; Eastern Yue thereupon deceived the King of Wu. When the King of Wu came out to review the troops, they had him speared to death, boxed his head, and galloped the relay post to report.
translations.idiomatic: Han bribed Eastern Yue; the Yue king betrayed Liu Bi. When he rode out to review the ranks, assassins ran him through, packed his head in a box, and raced the post-roads to the capital.

sentences.zh: 吳王太子駒亡走閩越。

translations.literal: The Wu heir Ju fled to Minyue.
translations.idiomatic: Crown prince Ju fled into Minyue.

sentences.zh: 吳王之棄軍亡也，軍遂潰，往往稍降太尉條侯及梁軍。

translations.literal: When the King of Wu abandoned his army and fled, the army thereupon collapsed; in many places they gradually surrendered to Grand Commandant Lord Tiaohou and to Liang’s forces.
translations.idiomatic: Once the king bolted, his army unraveled; bands here and there gave themselves up to Zhou Yafu or to Liang.

sentences.zh: 楚王戊軍敗，自殺。

translations.literal: King Wu of Chu, Liu Wu, was defeated in battle and committed suicide.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Wu, king of Chu, broke and killed himself.

sentences.zh: 三王之圍齊臨菑也，三月不能下。

translations.literal: When the three kings besieged Qi’s capital Linzi, they could not take it in three months.
translations.idiomatic: The triple alliance sat three months outside Linzi and could not storm it.

sentences.zh: 漢兵至，膠西、膠東、菑川王各引兵歸國。

translations.literal: When Han troops arrived, the kings of Jiaoxi, Jiaodong, and Zichuan each led their armies back to their kingdoms.
translations.idiomatic: Imperial columns approached; the kings of Jiaoxi, Jiaodong, and Zichuan peeled off and raced for home.

sentences.zh: 膠西王徒跣，席稿，飲水，謝太后。

translations.literal: The King of Jiaoxi went barefoot, sat on straw matting, drank only water, and apologized to the queen dowager.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Ang shed shoes, sat on rough straw, drank nothing but water, and begged his mother’s pardon.

sentences.zh: 王太子德曰：「漢兵還，臣觀之以罷，可襲，願收王餘兵擊之，不勝而逃入海，未晚也。」

translations.literal: The king's heir De said, “The Han army has returned; in my observation they are exhausted—we may strike. I beg to gather Your Majesty's remaining troops and attack them; if we do not prevail we can still flee to the sea—it will not be too late.”
translations.idiomatic: Prince De urged: “The Han host is spent from the march; strike now with what is left. If we lose, we can still run to the sea.”

sentences.zh: 王曰：「吾士卒皆已壞，不可用。」

translations.literal: The king said, “My soldiers are already broken and cannot be used.”
translations.idiomatic: The king answered, “My men are shattered—useless.”

sentences.zh: 不聽。

translations.literal: He would not listen.
translations.idiomatic: He refused.

sentences.zh: 漢將弓高侯頹當遺王書曰：「奉詔誅不義，降者赦，除其罪，復故；

translations.literal: Han general Marquis of Gonggao, Huidang, sent the king a letter saying, “By edict We punish the unrighteous; those who surrender shall be pardoned, their crimes removed, and their former status restored;
translations.idiomatic: Huidang of Gonggao wrote: “The throne punishes treason; yield and you are forgiven, your guilt wiped, your old rank returned;

sentences.zh: 不降者滅之。

translations.literal: those who do not submit shall be destroyed.”
translations.idiomatic: refuse and you are wiped out.”

sentences.zh: 王何處？

translations.literal: What will the king do?
translations.idiomatic: State your choice.”

sentences.zh: 須以從事。」

translations.literal: We await your word and will act accordingly.”
translations.idiomatic: Answer, and we shall proceed accordingly.”

sentences.zh: 王肉袒叩頭漢軍壁，謁曰：「臣卬奉法不謹，驚駭百姓，乃苦將軍遠道至于窮國，敢請葅醢之罪。」

translations.literal: The king bared his breast, knocked his head at the Han army rampart, and declared in audience, “Your servant Ang has not been careful in observing the law; I have terrified the common people and thus caused the general to come far to this exhausted state—I dare beg the crime of being minced for pickle.”
translations.idiomatic: Ang stripped to the chest, kowtowed at the Han lines, and said, “I broke the law, frightened the people, and dragged you to this poor corner—I deserve dismemberment.”

sentences.zh: 弓高侯執金鼓見之，曰：「王苦軍事，願聞王發兵狀。」

translations.literal: Marquis of Gonggao, holding drum and gong, received him and said, “The king has suffered in military affairs; I wish to hear how the king raised troops.”
translations.idiomatic: Huidang, drum and mallet in hand, asked, “You have borne the war—tell me how you came to arms.”

sentences.zh: 王頓首膝行對曰：「今者，朝錯天子用事臣，變更高皇帝法令，侵奪諸侯地。

translations.literal: The king, knocking his head and crawling on his knees, replied, “At present Chao Cuo is the minister in charge at the Son of Heaven's side; he has altered the laws of the August Emperor Gaozu and seized the princes' lands.
translations.idiomatic: On his knees Ang said, “Chao Cuo rules the court, rewrites Gaozu’s statutes, and gnaws at princely fiefs.

sentences.zh: 卬等以為不義，恐其敗亂天下，七國發兵，且以誅錯。

translations.literal: Ang and the others deemed this unrighteous and feared he would ruin and disorder the realm; therefore the seven states raised troops, intending for the time being to execute Chao Cuo.
translations.idiomatic: We judged that wicked and feared he would wreck the realm; the seven kingdoms rose chiefly to kill Cuo.

sentences.zh: 今聞錯已誅，卬等謹已罷兵歸。」

translations.literal: Now we hear Chao Cuo has already been executed; Ang and the others respectfully have already disbanded the army and returned.”
translations.idiomatic: Word came that Cuo is dead; we have stood down and gone home.”

sentences.zh: 將軍曰：「王苟以錯為不善，何不以聞？

translations.literal: The general said, “If the king truly thought Chao Cuo unworthy, why did you not report it?
translations.idiomatic: The general answered, “If Cuo was vile, why not memorialize the throne?

sentences.zh: 及未有詔虎符，擅發兵擊義國。

translations.literal: Moreover, before there was an edict or tiger tally, you on your own raised troops and attacked loyal principalities.
translations.idiomatic: You had no rescript, no tally, yet you mobilized and struck loyal states.

sentences.zh: 以此觀之，意非徒欲誅錯也。」

translations.literal: Judging by this, your intent was not merely to execute Chao Cuo.”
translations.idiomatic: That was never only about Cuo.”

sentences.zh: 乃出詔書為王讀之，曰：「王其自圖之。」

translations.literal: He then produced the edict and read it to the king, saying, “The king must decide for himself.”
translations.idiomatic: He drew out the imperial order and read it: “Decide your own fate.”

sentences.zh: 王曰：「如卬等死有餘罪。」

translations.literal: The king said, “For men like Ang death still leaves surplus guilt.”
translations.idiomatic: Ang said, “Men like me deserve more than one death.”

sentences.zh: 遂自殺。

translations.literal: Thereupon he committed suicide.
translations.idiomatic: He took his own life.

sentences.zh: 太后、太子皆死。

translations.literal: The queen dowager and the heir both died.
translations.idiomatic: His mother and heir died with him.

sentences.zh: 膠東、菑川、濟南王皆伏誅。

translations.literal: The kings of Jiaodong, Zichuan, and Jinan were all executed according to law.
translations.idiomatic: Jiaodong, Zichuan, and Jinan were put to the sword.

sentences.zh: 酈將軍攻趙，十月而下之，趙王自殺。

translations.literal: General Li attacked Zhao; in the tenth month he took it, and the King of Zhao committed suicide.
translations.idiomatic: Li Ji stormed Zhao in the tenth month; King Zhao killed himself.

sentences.zh: 濟北王以劫故，不誅。

translations.literal: The King of Jinbei, on account of having been coerced, was not executed.
translations.idiomatic: Jinbei was spared because he had been held hostage.

sentences.zh: 初，吳王首反，并將楚兵，連齊、趙。

translations.literal: Earlier, when the King of Wu first rebelled, he combined Chu’s troops and linked Qi and Zhao.
translations.idiomatic: At the start Liu Bi fired first, merged with Chu, and chained Qi and Zhao to his cause.

sentences.zh: 正月起，三月皆破滅。

translations.literal: They rose in the first month; by the third month all were broken and destroyed.
translations.idiomatic: They opened in the first month; by the third every kingdom lay in ruins.

sentences.zh: 贊曰：荊王王也，由漢初定，天下未集，故雖疏屬，以策為王，鎮江淮之間。

translations.literal: The appraisal says: The king of Jing was made king because, when Han was first settled and the realm not yet gathered, even though he was a distant kinsman, by policy he was made king to hold the ground between the Yangzi and the Huai.
translations.idiomatic: Ban Gu’s verdict: Jing was enfeoffed while the dynasty was still raw—distant blood, yet a deliberate chess piece to anchor the Huai–Yangzi belt.

sentences.zh: 劉澤發於田生，權激呂氏，然卒南面稱孤者三世。

translations.literal: Liu Ze rose through Tian Sheng, who by stratagem stirred the Lü clan, yet in the end faced south as solitary ruler for three generations.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Ze climbed on Tian Sheng’s intrigues against the Lüs, yet his line faced the throne for three reigns.

sentences.zh: 事發相重，豈不危哉！

translations.literal: When events erupted they pressed upon one another—was it not perilous!
translations.idiomatic: When one crisis triggered the next, the danger was beyond reckoning.

sentences.zh: 吳王擅山海之利，能薄斂以使其眾，逆亂之萌，自其子興。

translations.literal: The King of Wu monopolized the profits of mountains and seas, could lighten exactions and thereby command his people; the sprout of rebellion rose from his son.
translations.idiomatic: Wu hoarded salt and ore, bought the people with low taxes, and the seed of revolt was sown when his son died.

sentences.zh: 古者諸侯不過百里，山海不以封，蓋防此矣。

translations.literal: In antiquity princes did not exceed a hundred li, and mountains and seas were not granted as fiefs—presumably to guard against this.
translations.idiomatic: Ancient law capped a fief at a hundred li and barred the gift of mountains and seas—precisely to stop such power.

sentences.zh: 朝錯為國遠慮，禍反及身。

translations.literal: Chao Cuo planned far for the state, yet calamity rebounded on his own person.
translations.idiomatic: Chao Cuo thought for the realm’s long term and died for it.

sentences.zh: 「毋為權首，將受其咎」，豈謂錯哉！

translations.literal: ‘Do not be the first to seize power, or you will bear its blame’—does this not speak of Chao Cuo!
translations.idiomatic: “Never seize the helm of intrigue, or its guilt falls on you”—was that not Chao Cuo’s fate!
